text: The surgeon identifies a tattoo in the transverse colon, contrary to its described location in the cecum. The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon begins an extended right hemicolectomy by isolating the ileocolic vessels at the junction with the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and vein (SMV). The surgeon divides the vein first, followed by the artery, and performs medial dissection.

The surgeon locates the tattoo in the transverse colon, initially placed in the cecum. The robotic system is docked for an extended right hemicolectomy. The surgeon isolates the ileocolic vessels at the junction with the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and vein (SMV), dividing the vein first followed by the artery, and performs medial dissection.

The surgeon navigates up the superior mesenteric vessels towards the middle colic vessels, marking the mesenteric transection plan to maintain orientation. The right branch of the middle colic artery is visible. The surgeon isolates and divides the right colic artery and vein, then proceeds to address the middle colic pedicle.

The surgeon works along the superior mesenteric vessels towards the middle colic vessels, marking the mesenteric planned transection. The right branch of the middle colic is visible. The surgeon isolates and divides the right colic artery and vein, then proceeds to the middle colic pedicle, with the middle colic artery and vein coming into view for division.

The surgeon isolates and takes the middle colic artery, followed by the middle colic vein. The mesenteric vasculature is now transected. The surgeon proceeds to the planned distal margin, dissecting from the bowel wall down to the mesentery, taking the intervening mesentery and marginal branches.
The surgeon proceeds from the planned distal margin at the bowel wall, dissecting down to the mesenteric area, and excises the intervening mesentery along with the marginal branches.

The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the tumor, then performs lateral mobilization by taking down the gastrocolic ligament. The dissection continues around the hepatic flexure and down the lateral attachments to the right colon and cecum. The surgeon approaches from an inferior angle to complete the lateral attachments of the specimen.

The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the tumor, followed by lateral mobilization. The gastrocolic ligament is taken down, and the dissection proceeds around the hepatic flexure. The surgeon then detaches the lateral attachments to the right colon and cecum, approaching from an inferior angle to complete the mobilization of the specimen.

The surgeon takes the small bowel mesentery to the planned proximal margin, checking perfusion. The redundant transverse colon allows for stapling through the SuperP report despite the unexpected tumor location.

The surgeon takes the small bowel mesentery to the planned proximal margin, checking perfusion. The redundant transverse colon allows for stapling through the SuperP report despite the tumor's unexpected location.

The surgeon staples the small bowel and positions the specimen down to the pelvis for later extraction through a Pfannenstiel incision. An isoperistaltic anastomosis is lined up, with stay sutures placed at the far and near corners. A colotomy and enterotomy are performed, followed by stapling from the suprapubic port. The common channel is sewn closed starting at the near corner, using a transition stitch and a baseball stitch up to the far corner, then imbricating the tissue back down.
